questions.question: Storing a customer's email in every one of their order rows causes which problem?
options: An update anomaly: changing the email means updating many rows, and missing any creates contradictory data
The database runs out of primary keys
questions.explanation: Redundant data causes update anomalies. If the email is duplicated across a thousand order rows, a single change requires updating all of them; any missed row leaves the database with contradictory copies of the same fact.

questions.question: What does normalization fundamentally aim to do?
options: Store each fact exactly once, in the table it belongs to
questions.explanation: Normalization structures data so each fact lives in one place. This eliminates redundancy and the update/insert/delete anomalies it causes. Relationships are then resolved at read time with joins.

questions.question: What is the cost of denormalizing (e.g. copying customer_name into the orders table)?
options: You now have multiple copies of the same fact to keep in sync, risking data drift
questions.explanation: Denormalization trades write complexity for read speed. By storing redundant pre-joined data you skip the join on reads, but every change to the duplicated fact must update all copies — miss one and the data drifts out of sync.

questions.question: What does a foreign key enforce?
options: Referential integrity — you can't reference a row (e.g. a customer) that doesn't exist
questions.explanation: A foreign key points to another table's primary key and enforces referential integrity: the database rejects an order whose customer_id has no matching customer. This keeps relationships valid automatically.

questions.question: What is the recommended default approach to schema design?
options: Normalize first (each fact once); denormalize only for a specific hot read you've measured as too slow
Put everything in one wide table
Avoid foreign keys to keep writes fast
questions.explanation: Normalize by default — it's correct, flexible, and a well-indexed join is fast for most applications. Denormalize deliberately, only where a specific hot read is measurably too slow and you accept the consistency burden. Premature denormalization causes data-drift bugs.
